title: The Ceasefire That Wasn't
subhead: A week that began with US-Iran negotiations in Switzerland and a 60-day roadmap ended with American strikes on Iranian soil, Iranian retaliation against Gulf bases, and the June 17 ceasefire in tatters — all while a record heatwave killed hundreds in Europe, central banks turned hawkish in unison, and the US Supreme Court reshaped immigration enforcement.

sections.heading: The Ceasefire Unravels
sections.body:
The US-Iran ceasefire signed on June 17 barely survived the week. On Monday, JD Vance and Iranian parliamentary speaker Qalibaf held an 80-minute session in Switzerland, producing a 60-day roadmap and a Lebanon de-confliction cell. By Tuesday, the US lifted Iranian oil sanctions waivers for 60 days and Hormuz traffic began recovering.

Then the deal cracked. On Wednesday, the Republican-controlled Senate passed a war powers resolution to restrain Trump on Iran — a rare cross-party rebuke. On Thursday, Trump requested $87.6 billion for the Iran war, with $64 billion for the Pentagon, prompting bipartisan pushback. On Friday, Iran fired a drone at a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, the US retaliated with strikes on Iranian missile and drone sites on Qeshm Island — the first American strikes on Iranian soil since the ceasefire — and on Saturday, Iran struck back at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain. The ceasefire is now functionally over.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire was never solid — it was a pause in escalation that both sides used to reposition. The Swiss talks produced process, not peace, and the Hormuz attacks proved that Iran's IRGC can undercut any diplomatic track independently.

sections.heading: Hormuz: The Waterway That Broke Every Deal
sections.body: The Strait of Hormuz was the week's central battleground, militarily and economically. Iran shut the strait for the second time since the ceasefire MoU on Monday, tying its reopening to an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon. By midweek, transits were recovering — up to 20-25 ships per day — but the central channel remained mined (roughly 80 mines), keeping traffic at roughly 20% of pre-war levels. A Qatar-Barzan gas facility explosion on Tuesday killed 30 and cut LNG output by 30%. On Friday, an IRGC drone struck a Singapore-flagged cargo ship, prompting the IMO to suspend its evacuation of 11,000 stranded sailors. By Saturday, a second tanker was hit and roughly 500 ships remained stranded.
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz is the nexus where energy, military escalation, and trade chokepoints converge. Every attack on a ship in the strait instantly ripples through global oil markets, insurance rates, and supply chains — and the mines mean even a political ceasefire cannot restore normal traffic.

sections.heading: Central Banks Close Ranks Against Inflation
sections.body: This was the week central banks stopped pretending inflation was transitory. The Fed's Kevin Warsh presided over his first FOMC meeting with no forward guidance and a hawkish tilt — half the FOMC leaning toward rate hikes, with July hike bets hitting 36%. The Bank of Japan raised rates to 1% (a 31-year high) and signaled more tightening ahead. The ECB held at 2.25% but Philip Lane warned second-round inflation effects are sticky. US PCE inflation broke above 4.1% year-over-year in May, the highest since 2023. Gold crashed below $4,000 as the devaluation trade reversed.
sections.whyItMatters: Three major central banks moving hawkish simultaneously — while oil shocks from Hormuz add supply-side pressure — sets up a brutal second half of 2026. Rate hikes are coming at the same time sovereign debt costs are rising and fiscal spending on defense is surging.

sections.heading: Europe Burns: The Heatwave as Political Event
sections.body: A record heatwave killed at least 60 people in France, with temperatures exceeding 40°C across southern Europe. Portugal and Spain faced wildfire alerts, and the UK recorded its hottest June day. The heatwave compounded energy stress — cooling demand spiked natural gas consumption just as LNG supply from Qatar was disrupted by the Barzan explosion. France's power grid operator RTE warned of summer supply tightness. Meanwhile, climate policy took a back seat to crisis management across the continent.
sections.whyItMatters: The heatwave is not just a humanitarian crisis — it is a compounding shock on top of an energy system already strained by Hormuz disruptions, pushing European grids toward their limits and forcing governments to choose between cooling their populations and meeting climate targets.

sections.heading: Israel-Lebanon: A Framework Under Fire
sections.body: Israel and Lebanon announced a peace framework on Friday, mediated by the US, even as fighting continued on the ground. The framework emerged as a concession linked to the US-Iran negotiations — Iran had tied Hormuz reopening to an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon. Hezbollah lost senior commander Nabil Qaouk in an Israeli airstrike on Tuesday. Israel's security cabinet was split, with far-right ministers opposing any withdrawal. On the ground, Israeli operations in southern Lebanon continued throughout the week.
sections.whyItMatters: The Israel-Lebanon framework exists because the US needed it for the Iran track, not because the fighting has stopped. Hezbollah's command losses may actually increase operational unpredictability, and Israel's far-right coalition makes any territorial concession politically toxic.

sections.heading: The War Funding Fight
sections.body: Trump's $87.6 billion supplemental request for the Iran war — with $64 billion for the Pentagon — met immediate resistance from both parties. The Senate had already passed a war powers resolution to constrain the president's authority. Meanwhile, the Pentagon's base budget request for FY2027 came in at $876 billion, with $178 billion for acquisition alone. On the defense tech side, the Army moved to put critical minerals processing on military bases, and France seized its fifth Russian shadow fleet tanker. Ukraine's drone campaign pushed Russian refining to a two-decade low, forcing Moscow to import Indian gasoline.
sections.whyItMatters: Congress is signaling it will fund defense but wants oversight of war powers. The $87.6 billion request — on top of a record baseline defense budget — will force a debate about fiscal sustainability that connects directly to the central bank tightening cycle.

sections.heading: Trade Wars on Every Front
sections.body: Trump threatened a 100% tariff on countries imposing digital services taxes, targeting the UK, France, Italy, and others — opening a new transatlantic trade front even as the Iran war consumed attention. China hit back at US blacklists with rare earth export controls. Iraq threatened then retracted an OPEC exit threat over production quotas, after the UAE had already announced its departure. Russia weighed a diesel export ban as Ukrainian drones crippled its refineries. The UK seized a Russian shadow fleet tanker. Trump also told the EU it must buy more American energy or face tariffs.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is fighting trade wars on multiple fronts simultaneously — China on rare earths, Europe on digital taxes, and the Gulf on energy — while also prosecuting a hot war against Iran. Each escalates the others, and the cumulative effect on supply chains is compounding faster than any single dispute.

sections.heading: Supreme Court Reshapes Immigration, Tech Sovereignty Escalates
sections.body: The US Supreme Court delivered two rulings that will transform immigration enforcement — expanding federal power to deport and narrowing lower courts' ability to block removals. Separately, the court took up cases on state-level content moderation laws that could reshape how social media platforms operate. In tech, China imposed rare earth export controls targeting US defense firms, the Army moved critical minerals processing onto military bases, and Nvidia faced new export restrictions. The EU's AI Act enforcement stepped up with compliance deadlines, while OpenAI and Google both released new reasoning models in an intensifying AI race.
sections.whyItMatters: The Supreme Court's immigration rulings will have immediate and large-scale effects on enforcement. The rare earth controls and critical minerals militarization show how technology supply chains are being pulled into national security frameworks on all sides.

sections.heading: Venezuela's Earthquake and Colombia's Election
sections.body: A 6.8-magnitude earthquake struck Venezuela's Sucre state, killing over 1,400 people and displacing hundreds of thousands — the country's worst natural disaster in decades. The Maduro government's response was slow, and international aid was complicated by sanctions. In Colombia, Trump-backed outsider Miguel Uribe won the presidency by the slimmest margin in modern Colombian history, signaling a rightward shift in Latin America's third-largest economy. Bolivia lifted its state of emergency after blockades paralyzed the country for weeks.
sections.whyItMatters: Venezuela's earthquake compounds an already catastrophic humanitarian situation and creates an opening for either international engagement or further instability. Colombia's shift aligns it more closely with Washington at a time when US attention is consumed by the Middle East.

bottomLine: The June 17 US-Iran ceasefire is over. American strikes on Qeshm Island and Iranian retaliation against Kuwait and Bahrain have returned the conflict to active military escalation, with the Strait of Hormuz still mined and barely functional. Central banks across the G7 turned hawkish in unison, oil markets whipsawed, and a lethal heatwave compounded Europe's energy stress. Congress is pushing back on war powers even as the Pentagon requests $87.6 billion. The convergence of military escalation, monetary tightening, trade fragmentation, and climate crisis makes this the most volatile week since the war began — and none of these pressures are resolving.
sources: Frontion daily briefs 2026-06-22 through 2026-06-28 (global, energy, finance, defense, tech)
